2000-01-01.title: A Day at the Races, A Night of Big Parties
2000-01-01.abstract: How do you mark the passage of 1,000 years of human history? Simple in Hong Kong, a city of chronic gamblers and cold-eyed oddsmakers: you head for the track.    At 12:45 a.m., as the last wisps of smoke from New Year's Eve fireworks drifted away from the Happy Valley Racecourse, the crack of a starter's gun set off the Millennium Cup.

2000-01-01.title: Pakistan Welcomes Hijacking's End and Praises the Taliban
2000-01-01.abstract: Pakistan's government welcomes freeing of passengers held hostage aboard hijacked Indian Airlines plane in Afghanistan and praises that country's Taliban leaders for the way they managed the crisis (M)

2000-01-01.title: Well, Let's Try Again, Maybe Dec. 31, 2000
2000-01-01.abstract: At the last minute, Hungary's millennium planners turned into the Not-Ready-for-Prime-Time Players.    Two unusual events had been set for midnight: the first spin of a giant Wheel of Time holding an hourglass containing seven tons of customized sand, and a special session of Parliament to celebrate 1,000 years of Hungarian statehood.

2000-01-01.title: Hostages Land in India After Deal Is Made With Hijackers
2000-01-01.abstract: More and 150 people held hostage on a hijacked Indian Airlines plane for eight days arrive safely in New Delhi from Afghanistan after India secures their freedom by agreeing to release three jailed militants; five hijackers leave plan in Kandahar, Afghanistan, where they are joined by the released militants, who include a Muslim cleric named Maulana Masood Azhar; analysts say hijacking may have long-term political consequences in region, helping to legitimize Taliban, Afghan group that helped to end crisis, and further souring relations between India and Pakistan, which Indian officials and press have portrayed as hidden hand behind the hijacking; criticism in India over government's handling of hijacking intensifies in hours after hostages' release militants; photos (M)

2000-01-01.title: World Briefing
2000-01-01.abstract: EUROPE    FRANCE: MORE EXPLOSIVES LINKED TO BASQUES  The French police have seized a ton of explosives they said belonged to the Spanish Basque separatist guerrilla group E.T.A., a week after two powerful van bombs were found and defused in Spain. The explosives were found in an unused garage outside the city of Pau near the Spanish border.    Suzanne Daley (NYT)    BRITAIN: HONORS FOR ACTORS  Elizabeth Taylor, Julie Andrews and Sean Connery have new titles: Dame Elizabeth, Dame Julie and Sir Sean. The three actors were among 2,000 people honored in the annual New Year's list drawn up by the government and bestowed by the queen. Also honored were sports heroes, milkmen, crossing guards and the multimillionaire head of the Virgin Group, now Sir Richard Branson.   (AP)    AFRICA    SUDAN: CABINET QUITS  Three weeks after declaring a state of emergency, President Omar el-Bashir said he had accepted his cabinet's resignation, paving the way for him to consolidate his power. In a speech marking Sudan's 44th anniversary of independence, Mr. Bashir said ministers and state governors had expressed their desire to resign and he had accepted their resignations. However, he asked them to stay on as caretakers until he could reform the cabinet.   (AP)    NIGER: PREMIER AGAIN  A former prime minister, Hama Amadou, 49, was reappointed to the position by President Mamadou Tandja, who was sworn in on Dec. 22. Mr. Amadou also held the title in 1995, under President Mahamane Ousmane, who is now parliamentary speaker.    (Agence France-Presse)    CONGO: REBELS TELL OF AMBUSH  Rebels killed about 80 government soldiers in an ambush, a rebel leader said, a day after Congolese representatives held an unsuccessful meeting with a rebel envoy. Jean-Pierre Bemba, whose Congolese Liberation Movement is fighting to topple President Laurent Kabila with Ugandan backing, said government troops had been ambushed along the Congo River.    (AP)    ASIA    SRI LANKA: REBELS REPULSED  Troops repulsed Tamil Tiger rebels trying to overrun defenses at a key northern military base, the defense ministry said. The ministry said 9 soldiers and 9 rebels had been killed and 41 soldiers hurt, although independent confirmation of the figures was not possible.    (Reuters)    EAST TIMOR: U.N. AIDE APOLOGIZES  The United Nations administrator in East Timor apologized for its failure to predict and prevent a wave of violence after the territory's overwhelming vote for independence from Indonesia. Sergio Vieira de Mello, who is overseeing East Timor's transition to independence, made the apology during a visit to the coffee-growing area of Ermera, said the United Nations spokesman Fred Eckhard.   (AP)    EAST TIMOR: GUSMAO RULES HIMSELF OUT  Jose Alexandre Gusmao said he foresaw full independence for East Timor in at most two years but that he did not plan to become its first president. Mr. Gusmao had been widely expected to become an independent East Timor's first leader, but said he intended to signal that leaders of the independence struggle did not have an automatic right to power.    (Agence France-Presse)    THE AMERICAS    HONDURAS, NICARAGUA: MILITARY PACT  Honduras and Nicaragua have agreed to freeze ground troop deployments and withdraw naval forces from the Caribbean Sea to avert military conflict pending resolution of a border dispute, an official with the Organization of American States said. Relations soured in November when the Honduran Congress ratified an accord that claimed for Honduras and Colombia 52,000 square miles of Caribbean coastal waters, parts of which are also claimed by Nicaragua.    (Reuters)    PARAGUAY: CABINET MINISTER QUITS  Agriculture Minister Oscar Denis quit after his Liberal Party voted to abandon the coalition government of President Luis Gonzalez Macchi. The party's leadership voted to pull out after talks to obtain more government posts failed. The decision faces ratification by party members at a convention in February.    (Reuters)    COLOMBIA: LEADER VETOES GENOCIDE BILL  In an unex-pected reversal, President Andres Pastrana vetoed a bill that would have made genocide and forced disappearance criminal acts. His administration shifted course after the armed forces complained that such a law would leave soldiers open to prosecution, in civilian rather than military courts, for deaths inflicted in combat with leftist guerrillas.    Larry Rohter (NYT)    MIDDLE EAST    IRAN: CALL TO DESTROY ISRAEL  Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the supreme leader, called for the destruction of Israel, saying it was the only way to solve the Middle East's problems. His remarks came as millions of people rallied across Iran to protest Israel's control of Jerusalem, where Muslims believe Muhammad began his journey to heaven.    (AP)    LEBANON: HEZBOLLAH'S VOW  The Hezbollah movement said it intended to keep up its attacks on Israeli troops in south Lebanon, a day after a suicide bombing narrowly missed an Israeli patrol near the village of Qlaia, wounding 12 Lebanese civilians and an Israeli soldier. ''Hezbollah operations will not stop before or after the negotiations'' between Israel and Syria, a Hezbollah press official said.    (Agence France-Presse)

2000-01-02.title: Diners's Taste Leads to Cat-Nabbing in Beijing
2000-01-02.abstract: Family cats are being stolen in Beijing to meet the demand for cat meat in Cantonese-style restaurants, which have been opening up across the city (M)

2000-01-02.title: Hostages Recall Times of Terror Amid Boredom
2000-01-02.abstract: Passengers and crew kept hostage for eight days aboard a hijacked Indian Airlines jetliner describe days of tedium and moments of terror; talk of living with stench of overflowing toilets and of deciding to stop eating solid foods in an effort to avoid the dreaded bathrooms; some admit to strange bond they formed with one hijacker, who was the most sympathetic and solicitous until the day he told them that they would all be killed; photos (M)

2000-01-02.title: With Fears Of Terrorism, Precautions Will Continue
2000-01-02.abstract: Law enforcement and intelligence officials in US and around the world remain on high state of alert because of possibility of terrorism, even as they express relief that year 2000 began without any sign of significant disruption caused by criminal or extremist groups (M)

2000-01-02.title: IN A BUSY START, NEW RUSSIA LEADER GOES TO CHECHNYA
2000-01-02.abstract: Acting Pres Vladimir V Putin celebrates New Year with Russian troops fighting in Chechnya; visit offers nation used to Boris N Yeltsin a new image of an active, concerned leader and reflects Putin's close association with Chechnya battle; his success in presidential elections now scheduled for March 26 could well hinge on success of campaign; photo (M)

2000-01-02.title: Chechnya's Capital Becomes A Deadly Zone for Russians
2000-01-02.abstract: Grozny, capital of Chechnya, becomes a deadly killing zone as Russian troops run into fierce resistance from heavily armed, experienced and motivated rebels; make inroads, but still do not control city center; official casuality figures understate Russian losses, which are estimated at 10 soldiers a day; popularity of Vladimir V Putin, acting president, is based in large part reports of low casualties; photos; map (M)

2000-01-03.title: SHADOWS OF TORMENT/A special report.; Pinochet Case Reviving Voices of the Tortured
2000-01-03.abstract: It was only when Mario Fernandez saw the headline, ''Pinochet Under Arrest,'' that the dam broke and he finally found it possible to talk about the beatings, the electric shocks, the cigarette burns, the terrible sense of humiliation and alienation.    ''My body froze; I had an intense allergic reaction, and I didn't know whether to laugh or cry,'' said Mr. Fernandez, trembling at the memory. He ran to his wife and wept on her shoulder, and, at long last, took her advice to seek therapy.

2000-01-03.title: Clinton's Final Chapter: Single-Minded, Full-Steam Run at a Global Agenda
2000-01-03.abstract: For much of his presidency, Bill Clinton's own aides have frequently complained that his attention to foreign policy was sporadic. The trait got him in trouble in Bosnia, and often resulted in some nasty surprises -- from the defeat of the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty last fall to a tear-gas-filled week in Seattle that badly damaged his global economic agenda.    But some of his biggest victories in 1999 were also scored abroad, from the air campaign over Serbia that largely halted ethnic bloodshed to the Northern Ireland accord that offers the first prospect of peace there.

2000-01-03.title: Kosovo Inquiry Confirms U.S. Fears of War Crimes Court
2000-01-03.abstract: Eighteen months ago the Pentagon strongly opposed a plan to create a permanent international criminal tribunal, fearing that a strong and independent court would expose American troops sent overseas to prosecution outside the American judicial system.    Now, as a result of NATO's air war against the Serbs last spring, members of the American military have already undergone the scrutiny of just such a court: the International Criminal Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia. Experts on international law say that may create political and legal precedents for requiring Americans to submit to international judicial review.

2000-01-03.title: Argentina's Former President Still at Center Stage
2000-01-03.abstract: As president, Carlos Saul Menem dominated Argentine news coverage for more than a decade by selling off much of the government and pegging the peso to the dollar -- not to mention racing sports cars, throwing his wife out of the presidential palace and dating women half his age.     Now out of power for barely three weeks, Mr. Menem has managed to remain at the center of attention. He has sometimes even managed to overshadow the always demure President Fernando de la Rua, who campaigned promising a new style of government.

2000-01-03.title: ISRAEL AND SYRIA MEET TODAY IN U.S. ON TANGLED ISSUES
2000-01-03.abstract: Having done little more than break the ice in their first encounter last month, Israeli and Syrian leaders enter knottier terrain on Monday as they resume American-brokered negotiations ambitiously aimed at reaching a peace accord by the summer.    The mere fact of their meeting was achievement enough when the Israelis and the Syrians revived their peace effort in December after a four-year stalemate. This time the two sides will be forced to tackle directly the tangle of issues that have bedeviled their previous efforts at peacemaking: borders, security and the opening of normal relations.

2000-01-04.title: Putin Takes Charge, Firing Yeltsin's Daughter and Others
2000-01-04.abstract: Acting President Vladimir V. Putin began to put his own imprint on the Kremlin administration today, showing the door to Tatyana Dyachenko, who wielded considerable influence in the administration of her father, Boris N. Yeltsin.    Mr. Putin also moved aside several other staff members who had been loyal to Mr. Yeltsin. But as one television commentator said tonight, the changes amount to a ''freshening up'' of the administration rather than a revolution inside the Kremlin.

2000-01-04.title: Growing Market Offers Huge Potential — but Also Peril : Globalization's Double Edge
WORLD BRIEFING
2000-01-04.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: NAZI SUSPECT DEPORTED -- Britain ordered the deportation to Australia of Konrad Kalejs, 86, suspected of being a Nazi death squad officer responsible for the murder of up to 30,000 Jews, Communists and Gypsies in Latvia in World War II. Mr. Kalejs, an Australian citizen of Latvian descent, was found living in a retirement home in England. The police said they had no grounds for arresting Mr. Kalejs and Australia said it had already decided not to prosecute him.   Sarah Lyall (NYT)    FRANCE: OUSTED PRESIDENT ARRIVES -- The former President of the Ivory Coast, Henri Konan Bedie, who was ousted in a military coup on Dec. 24, arrived in Paris. He had been staying in Togo since the coup and flew into Orly airport aboard the Togolese presidential jet. Togolese officials said Mr. Bedie, who has an apartment in Paris, is expected to take up residence there.   Suzanne Daley (NYT)

2000-01-04.title: OBITUARY : Alan Jolis, 46, Author
2000-01-04.abstract: Alan Jolis, 46, the author most recently of "Love and Terror," a historical novel set during the French Revolution, died Thursday at his home in Sweden after a long struggle with brain cancer.

2000-01-04.title: Gomitogo Journal; Undependable Rains Bring Seasonal Exodus
2000-01-04.abstract: Slumped in the shadow of a mud wall, any desire he might have had to stand up drained by the midday sun and the Ramadan fast, Mamadou Tombo explained the simple truth about his village of rice farmers.    ''When there was good flood and rain,'' said Mr. Tombo, the mayor, ''there was no exodus.''

2000-01-05.title: Israel to Give Palestinians Another Piece Of West Bank
2000-01-05.abstract: Israel will transfer another chunk of the West Bank to the Palestinians by Thursday after negotiators resolved a lingering dispute over the land today, ending a stalemate that had dispirited both sides.    After the maps were signed tonight, Israel was expected to turn over 5 percent of the West Bank to the Palestinian Authority within 48 hours. Most of the land is an unpopulated swath of the Judean Desert.

2000-01-05.title: Russia's Presidential Race Lacks Runners
2000-01-05.abstract: The nagging question today, as Russian officials began laboriously assembling the machinery for Russia's second post-Soviet presidential election, was not how or when the vote would be held. It was why.    With less than 12 weeks remaining until the likeliest election date in late March, not a single serious politician has firmly committed to running against acting President Vladimir V. Putin. Commentators are rating the prospects of anyone who does as close to zero.

2000-01-05.title: Clinton Breaks an Impasse At the Israel-Syria Talks
2000-01-05.abstract: President Clinton made a second trip here today, breaking an impasse between Syria and Israel over the agenda for their peace talks and convening the first face-to-face talks between Prime Minister Ehud Barak of Israel and Foreign Minister Farouk al-Shara of Syria.     Mr. Clinton's reappearance in this small West Virginia town came after Israeli and Syrian negotiators bogged down in a dispute over whether the first topic of discussion would be Syria's demand for the return of the Golan Heights, which Israel has controlled since the 1967 Middle East war, or Israel's search for security guarantees.

2000-01-05.title: Pro-Western Opposition Defeats Croatia's Ruling Nationalists
2000-01-05.abstract: Croatia's center-left opposition won a sweeping victory in parliamentary elections on Monday, official results indicated today. The elections delivered a rebuke to the governing party and opens up the prospect of much warmer ties with the West.    The opposition victory dethroned the Croatian Democratic Union, the umbrella nationalist party founded by Franjo Tudjman, the president who died last month. The party has ruled Croatia since Mr. Tudjman took the country to independence in 1991 out of the wreckage of Tito's Yugoslavia.

2000-01-05.title: Lijiang Journal; A Dying Culture, as Seen Through Aging Eyes
2000-01-05.abstract: The two old men live in a small research center in this stunning mountain town, translating religious scriptures of their minority culture from a pictographic script that almost nobody else can fully decipher anymore.    These men are the last of the dongba -- priests of the Naxi people who have lived in Lijiang and the surrounding mountains of southwestern China with their own animist religion and writing system for more than 1,000 years. Now they are helping to compile an accessible record of their sacred knowledge before it dies out altogether.

2000-01-06.title: Dow Rebounds as World Watches : Asian Markets Join European Sell-Off
2000-01-06.abstract: In some of the sharpest market movements since the Asian financial crisis ended last year, stock prices across Asia and Europe plunged Wednesday in response to major falls in U.S. markets Tuesday.

2000-01-06.title: Croatian Victors Fret About What Awaits Them
2000-01-06.abstract: In the midst of the euphoria and high expectations following their convincing victory in parliamentary elections on Monday, leaders of Croatia's democratic opposition are growing concerned about when they will be able to take power and what they will find when they do so.     Ivica Racan, the moderate former Communist who will become the country's new prime minister, said in an interview today that it could be a month or more before Croatia's cumbersome constitutional process  allows him to form a government.

2000-01-06.title: In No Hurry, Israel-Syria Talks Edge Toward Specifics
2000-01-06.abstract: On the third day of talks, Israeli and Syrian officials today slowly began tackling specific questions of how they might normalize relations.    They also took up security arrangements, with an eye to persuading Israeli voters to approve a return of the Golan Heights.

2000-01-06.title: Chechen Rebels Fiercely Attack Russian Forces
2000-01-06.abstract: Encircled by Russian forces and subjected to constant bombardment, Chechen rebels in Grozny have begun to lash back, forcing furious firefights on the outskirts of the city.    Rebel officials asserted today that they had left their hiding places in Grozny and seized the village of Alkhan-Kala, on the southwestern outskirts of the Chechen capital, according to independent media outlets in Russia.

2000-01-06.title: Crime Kindles Rights Debate In South Africa
2000-01-06.abstract: Angered by several bombings in Cape Town and frustrated by the difficulties in solving such cases, South Africa is considering constitutional changes that would curb the rights of suspected terrorists.    The country's safety and security minister, Steve Tshwete, wants the police to have the power to hold such suspects without charges for more than the 48 hours now allowed and wants to curtail their access to lawyers, law enforcement officials say.

2000-01-07.title: Somosomo Journal; Island of the Endless Smile, but With Sharks, Too
2000-01-07.abstract: On his knees, the herald bent toward the acting chief, fingering his gift of a whale's tooth as he murmured the standard apologies of greeting.    He had come, he said, to introduce a foreigner who asked leave to wander around the village, and begged forgiveness in advance for any breaches of protocol.

2000-01-07.title: INDIA  INTENSIFIES HIJACKING DISPUTE
2000-01-07.abstract: India today intensified its efforts to blame Pakistan for the Indian Airlines hijacking, announcing that four accomplices had been seized in Bombay and that they had identified the five hijackers as Pakistanis and acknowledged official Pakistani sponsorship of the hijacking.    The home minister, Lal Krishna Advani, said at a news conference here that the statements of the four reported accomplices -- an Indian, two Pakistanis and a Nepali -- and other ''telltale pointers'' conclusively established Pakistan's role in the seizure of the Airbus. The plane was hijacked as it flew from Katmandu, Nepal, to New Delhi on Dec. 24 with 178 passengers and 11 crew members on board.

2000-01-07.title: Paris Soccer Team Struggles to De-Nazify a Cheering Section
2000-01-07.abstract: PARIS—  For a good part of the last two decades, France has lived with a nasty, neo-fascist pustule in a highly visible place: R1 Rouge, a section of grandstand virtually turned into an enclave for young, right-wing extremists at Parc des Princes, the soccer stadium of Paris St. Germain.

2000-01-07.title: Yeltsin's Visit To Holy Land Is a Portrait Of Paradox
2000-01-07.abstract: Boris N. Yeltsin's first pilgrimage to the Holy Land offered up a series of curious juxtapositions today: the man who entered politics as a Communist received a high honor from a senior Orthodox cleric; Russia's first president pronounced his historically anti-Semitic land free of anti-Jewish sentiment; the convert to democracy who has just quit politics sat at lunch opposite Natan Sharansky, a major political force in Israel, who spent nine years in Soviet jails defending freedoms that Mr. Yeltsin only later brought to his country.    Everywhere he went, Mr. Yeltsin was treated like the head of state he so recently was, before his stunning resignation on New Year's Eve. And everywhere, church and political leaders eagerly sought his embrace.

2000-01-07.title: Beijing-Backed Church, Defying Vatican, Installs 5 New Bishops
2000-01-07.abstract: In a move that the Vatican says will damage efforts to mend its relations with Beijing, China's state-sanctioned Catholic Church consecrated five new bishops here today, just hours before Pope John Paul II consecrated 12 bishops at St. Peter's Basilica.    The two-hour ceremony, on a wintry morning in the unheated Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception here, adhered closely to the rites of a traditional Catholic consecration. But the bishops, who range in age from 35 to 73, were appointed not by the Vatican, but by the Patriotic Catholic Association -- a sort of parallel church that answers to the Chinese government.

2000-01-08.title: Short Takes
2000-01-08.abstract: After years of rapid growth, U.S. waste-recycling efforts are leveling off. The percentage of municipal solid waste diverted to recycling rose from 10 percent in 1980 to 28.8 percent in 1998 before flattening out, The Washington Post reports.

2000-01-08.title: Clinton Offers Israel and Syria 7-Page 'Working Paper' to Study Golan Heights Control
2000-01-08.abstract: Pres Clinton intervenes in Israeli-Syrian peace talks, offering seven-page 'working paper' defining differences over deal that would return Golan Heights to Syria; acts after American officials find that Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak and Syrian Min Farouk al-Shara have sufficiently shown to domestic constituencies that they are holding out on key issues; photo (M)

2000-01-08.title: Thousands Protesting In Indonesia Threaten An Islamic Holy War : Muslims Call On Jakarta To Intervene In Moluccas
2000-01-08.abstract: SINGAPORE—  Tens of thousands of Indonesian Muslims demonstrated in Jakarta on Friday for the third day in a row to press the government to end sectarian violence in the Moluccan Islands.

2000-01-08.title: In Ancestral Russia Ruins, Family Asserts Its Rights
2000-01-08.abstract: Prince Yevgeny A Meshchersky and family have been squatting for three years in ruined gatehouse, Alabino, Russia, in bid to reclaim ancestral 64-acre estate; authorities have done nothing to evict him or to give him property he claims; he comments, interview; map; photo (M)

2000-01-08.title: U.S. Helps Russia Turn Germ Center to Peace Uses
2000-01-08.abstract: Clinton administration officials and scientists who have toured Vector, Siberian lab formerly used by Soviet Union to develop viral weapons, say quiet United States aid to convert lab to peaceful research seems to be paying off; some American intelligence analysts and Congressional staff members remain wary; photo (M)

2000-01-09.title: Chilean's Homespun Record Could Make Him President
2000-01-09.abstract: Joaquin Lavin, first conservative candidate with chance to win Chile's presidency since Gen Augusto Pinochet left power in 1990, has softened image of country's right wing, which still carries baggage of human rights abuses of Pinochet dictatorship; Lavin, mayor of Las Condes, is part of new wave of younger politicians who appear committed to overturning traditionally highly centralized power structures of Latin y America, parlaying local government success into capturing national attention, and even high office; photo (M)

2000-01-09.title: Czech Poll: Harassment Of Women Is Common
2000-01-09.abstract: Recent study commissioned by Czech daily indicates that almost half of working women in Czech Republic have been subjected to sexual harassment in workplace; reports from other post-Communist countries indicate that sexual harassment, though seldom identified as such, is widespread; in Czech Republic, 45 percent of women say they were sexually harassed, many of them repeatedly; women's rights advocates say real figures are probably higher; note that term often used by Czechs for sexual harassment, translated, means 'sexual buzz,' with tacit understanding that whoever complains about harassment is making unnecessary fuss (M)

2000-01-09.title: After Crisis, Reform Bills Languish In Brazil
2000-01-09.abstract: Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso summons lawmakers back to Brasilia for special session to consider his 2000 budget; congressional leaders, mindful of local elections, have dismissed his austerity package and countered with proposals of their own that would limit presidential powers and dip further into federal purse; just one year ago, currency crisis in Brazil sparked fears that country's economy was headed toward meltdown; growth has resumed, and it is once again business as usual in Congress (M)

2000-01-09.title: As Russian Army Rests, New Spins Offered for Lull in Grozny
2000-01-09.abstract: Acting Pres Vladimir V Putin says decision to suspend Russia's bombardment of Grozny was taken to spare civilians and to honor Orthodox and Muslim holidays; Putin's reasons for halting bombardment are different from military's, suggesting that announcement is at least partly matter of packaging and public relations; suspension appears to be prelude to even more furious assault on Chechen capital; photo (M)

2000-01-09.title: STATE UNDER SIEGE: A special report.; Mexican Tale of Absolute Drug Corruption
2000-01-09.abstract: State Under Seige, special report on how illicit drug trade now defines lives of residents of Baja California; for many years Mexicans believed that drugs like cocaine merely passed through their country en route to United States; now street crime, fed by explosion of drug abuse, has risen exponentially in Tijuana and elsewhere; police are overwhelmed by drug-related killings and courts awash in traffickers' bribes; crimes like kidnappings, bank robberies and drive-by shootings are commonplace; public safety is biggest concern of citizens; they thought that 10 years ago, when they voted out corrupt political machine, there would be new era; but waves of drug-related crime that coincided with more democratic politics is eroding their faith that any government can improve their lives; major presidential candidates cite collapsing justice system as crucial issue, but they say little to sustain faith in power of free political choice; photos; map (L)

2000-01-10.title: Hans Schwerte, 90, Ex-SS Man Who Hid Identity
2000-01-10.abstract: Hans Schwerte, an authority on literary history who admitted in 1995 that under another name he had been an SS officer, died on Dec. 18 in a retirement home in Marquartstein, Germany. He was 90. His death was announced by the Aachen Technical University.     By the time that he acknowledged his SS role under his original name, Hans-Ernst Schneider, Dr. Schwerte had retired as a professor at the university, where he was rector from 1970 to 1973, and had received a high German decoration for his postwar contributions to German scholarship.

2000-01-10.title: Putin Was Once Decorated as a Spy; Few Agree on His Deeds
2000-01-10.abstract: A spate of reports in the German press suggested this weekend that President Vladimir V. Putin was decorated for his work as a K.G.B. agent in East Germany during the 1980's, and was even expelled from West Germany at one point after being identified as a Soviet spy.     But beyond vague statements from German intelligence spokesmen, there was little to confirm the accounts. American intelligence officials, speaking privately, have said that to their knowledge, Mr. Putin's 15-year career as a K.G.B. agent was not exceptional.

2000-01-10.title: An 1862 Battle Offers Impetus for 'Tough' Mideast Talks
2000-01-10.abstract: On what appeared to be the last full day of an initial round of peace talks between Israel and Syria, Prime Minister Ehud Barak of Israel on Sunday took a lengthy tour of the site of one of the deadliest  single confrontations in American history, the battle of Antietam, and spent the evening at a final dinner with President Clinton and Syria's foreign minister hashing out a path to an elusive peace deal.     Mr. Clinton's dinner and then separate meetings with the leaders lasted until 12:30 a.m. today, and as the president returned to the White House the State Department spokesman, James P. Rubin, said, ''All the issues came up at dinner.''

2000-01-10.title: Russian Troops in Chechnya Find Little Quiet on the Southern Front
2000-01-10.abstract: ''Artillery ready?'' Lt. Col. Yuri Budanov shouted.    ''On the planned targets!'' came the reply.

2000-01-10.title: Leon Radzinowicz, 93, Leader in Criminology
2000-01-10.abstract: Leon Radzinowicz, a scholar of criminology who played a major role in the development of the field, died on Dec. 29 at his home in Haverford, Pa. He was 93.    For most of his career, Mr. Radzinowicz, who was knighted by Queen Elizabeth II in 1970, taught law at Cambridge University, where he also founded the Institute of Criminology in 1959; he retired in 1973 after serving as its first director.

2000-01-11.title: Spy Photos of Korea Missile Site Bring Dispute
2000-01-11.abstract: Analysts clash over images of North Korea's Rodong missile site taken by private spy satellite and quietly released last week by Space Imaging Inc; some dismiss site as surprisingly primitive, while others express concern that so little infrastructure is needed to launch deadly missiles; company president Jeffrey K Harris says first detailed public images of site contribute to informed debate; photos; map (M)

2000-01-11.title: Troops Try to Regain Footing in Chechnya After Rebel Strikes
2000-01-11.abstract: Russian forces battle to restore control behind their front lines after surprise rebel attacks that apparently throw Russian forces off balance and raise questions about Russian strategy in Chechnya; Defense Min Igor Sergeyev ends suspension of air and artillery attacks on Grozny and resumes full-scale offensive; imposes evening curfew on rebellious republic; map; photo (M)

2000-01-11.title: Gore Presides Over Rare Security Council Debate on AIDS
2000-01-11.abstract: Vice Pres Al Gore presides during presidential primary campaign over unusual United Nations Security Council session on AIDS crisis in Africa; says United States will increase spending by $150 million to combat infectious diseases in poorest nations; World Bank president, James D Wolfensohn, warns epidemic is more effective than war in destabilizing nations; experts say at least 70 percent of over 33 million people infected with AIDS virus are African; Sec Gen Kofi Annan cites need to smash wall of silence and stigma surrounding AIDS; US Amb Richard C Holbrooke organized debate; graphs of causes of death and declining life expectancy in Africa; photo (M)

2000-01-11.title: Clinton to Offer Aid in Colombia Drug War
2000-01-11.abstract: Pres Clinton plans $1.28 billion emergency aid program to help Colombia fight growing drug trade and to prop up its democracy over next two years; much of money would be spent on military hardware to upgrade Colombian forces battling narcotics traders, many of whom are aligned with antigovernment rebels; funds would also be used to help strengthen justice system and aid people displaced by civil war (M)

2000-01-11.title: Foes Fight Ecuador's Leader on Dollar Plan
2000-01-11.abstract: Opposition groups score Ecuador Pres Jamil Mahuad's bid to solve economic crisis by adopting United States dollar as new currency; warn of 'massive impoverishment;' threaten to paralyze nation until he resigns; Mahaud also has asked cabinet to resign (M)

2000-01-12.title: Barak Says Talks With Syria Have Reached Critical Juncture
2000-01-12.abstract: Prime Min Ehud Barak returns to Israel and reports that peace talks with Syria have reached 'decisive stage' in which both sides will have to make difficult decisions, television interview; sees 'certain fissures' in Syrian rigidity but is not reading too much into them; dismisses concerns that Pres Hafez al-Assad shows disrespect by sending Foreign Min Farouk al-Shara, and declines to make issue of whether or not he and Shara shake hands; says no in-depth talks on borders have taken place and Israelis have made no political commitment on withdrawal from Golan Heights (S)

2000-01-12.title: Mexico City Journal; Can't Find Juarez Street? There Are Hundreds!
2000-01-12.abstract: Mexico City Journal on confusion of Mexico City street names; photo; map; developers each year carve out some 1,500 new streets, another 300 are renamed because honorees have fallen out of favor; 1998 survey found 80,000 blocks lack signs, thousands of avenues have no official name, and others carry names of politicians in ill repute; favorite names are repeated dozens of times; current review appears prompted by 17 streets named for Carlos Salinas de Gortari, former president associated with plunder of public treasury (M)

2000-01-12.title: A German-Canadian Fights Extradition: He's a Key Figure in Kohl Case
2000-01-12.abstract: Karlheinz Schreiber, millionaire German arms broker, is fighting extradition from Canada to testify in corruption investigation of former Chancellor Helmut Kohl; Schreiber acknowledges handing envelope stuffed with one million marks to Christian Democratic emissaries in Swiss restaurant in 1991, but says it was campaign money, not bribe; photo (M)

2000-01-12.title: Chechen Rebels Swim in Friendly Waters to Nip Russians
2000-01-12.abstract: Russian commanders in Shali, Chechnya, provide first on-the-scene account of fighting that has alarmed authorities in Moscow and prompted close review of strategy in breakaway province; photos; map; suprise offensive by Chechens in Shali and Argun recounted; Defense Min Igor Sergeyev says situation has radically changed (M)

2000-01-12.title: Kohl, a Stubborn Statesman, May Be Wrecking His Party
2000-01-12.abstract: Streak of obstinacy that helped Helmut Kohl unify Germany and persuade his countrymen to abandon their beloved mark for euro now appears to be unhinging his Christian Democratic party and unraveling Kohl's reputation; Kohl has opened devastating party divisions by admitting he accepted over $1 million in secret payments to party but refusing to reveal donors' names or leave politics in face of criminal investigation; Wolfgang Schauble, current party leader and Kohl protege, now admits to taking $52,000 in cash in 1994 from Karlheinz Schreiber, arms broker now fighting extradition from Canada; Schauble says he passed money along to party treasurer Brigitte Baumeister; Schauble has until now tried to distance himself from affair, but neither he nor Kohl appear to have entirely grasped gravity of scandal; photo (M)

2000-01-13.title: Shelburne Journal; Tales of Sex, Violence and Greed in a Small Town
2000-01-13.abstract: Lee Keating, former supervisor at Shelburne School for Boys, reformatory in Nova Scotia, is among 84 former employees who object to decision of Nova Scota provincial government to accept largely on face value charges of former residents of school that they were physically or sexually abused by staff members, and to pay them from $25 million compensation fund for victims; one former employee was convicted of sex abuse in 1993, after which many suits were filed by former residents against virtually entire staff; Keating and others say they are victims of witch hunt; photos; map (M)

2000-01-13.title: OBITUARY : Henry Pleasants, 89, Music Critic, Dies
2000-01-13.abstract: Henry Pleasants, 89, music critic, author and historian of the singing voice, died in a London hospital Jan. 4 after suffering a ruptured aorta. He had lived in London for more than 30 years.

2000-01-13.title: Mandela's Next Job: Trying to Cool One of Africa's Hottest Spots
2000-01-13.abstract: Nelson Mandela, former South African president, will attend Security Council debate on worsening political and economic situation in Burundi; Mandela is new international mediator for Burundi; at center of debate is Burundi's 'regroupment' policy under which Tutsi-led government of Pres Pierre Buyoya has forcibly resettled 300,000 mostly ethnic Hutus in dozens of spartan camps (M)

2000-01-13.title: Luxury Sportswear Casually Trumps the Suit
2000-01-13.abstract: MILAN—  As dress codes in the workplace continue to ease up, the race is on to find a comfortable alternative to the conventional suit and a new generation of designers see sportswear as the best solution.

2000-01-13.title: British, Under European Ruling, End Ban on Openly Gay Soldiers
2000-01-13.abstract: British government ends its ban on openly gay men and women serving in armed forces, following European court ruling that Britain could no longer keep the exclusion; this ends Britain's decades-old practice of subjecting gays in military to intrusive investigations and dishonorable discharges; change brings Britain into line with almost all other NATO nations, which allow openly gay men and lesbians to serve in their armed forces; United States, with its 'don't ask, don't tell' policy, is at variance with trend; Britain's about-face became inevitable after European Court of Human Rights unanimously ruled last September in favor of four former servicemen and servicewomen who were discharged in mid-1990's because of their homosexuality; photo (M)

2000-01-14.title: U.N. Studies How Refugees Qualify to Get Assistance
2000-01-14.abstract: Security Council is confronting issue of how new wars are demanding new definitions and new policies if millions of people they have made homeless are to survive; refugee experts say 20 million or more people have abandoned their houses, driven forcibly by governments and guerrilla armies or voluntarily in search of safety; because they remain in their own countires, they fall outside purview of organization's high commissioner for refugees; high commissioner, Sadako Ogata, has pushed hard to include internally displaced within her organization's reach, thereby making them eligible for international aid; United States representative, Richard C Holbrooke, has urged council to rethink how refugee organization could be retooled to meet crisis that exists around world (M)

2000-01-14.title: Russia Takes Chechen Town, but Can It Keep It?
2000-01-14.abstract: Russian military leaders say they have again taken control of Argun from Chechen rebels, but soldiers moving through are nervous about snipers and clearly do not think all danger has passed; Argun has become conspicuous example of how hard it may be for Russia to keep what it has taken; some officers doubt that Argun, first taken by Russians in early December, would ever be totally secure; Russian commandant of town, Col Vladimir N Kushnaryov, is gunned down after his armored column is surrounded; photo; map (M)

2000-01-14.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-01-14.abstract: ASIA    INDIA: EX-STATE CHIEF CLEARED  Jayalalitha Jayaram, the former actress and onetime chief minister of the state of Tamil Nadu, won an important victory in a criminal case when charges against her in a reportedly crooked land deal were dropped. The same state court also upheld an earlier ruling that absolved her of guilt in a reported coal-importing scam. Two other charges against her are still pending.   Barry Bearak (NYT)    MALAYSIA: CRACKDOWN CONTINUES  The government extended a crackdown on opposition figures, arresting another top political leader and charging two people with sedition. The latest arrest, the fifth since Wednesday, was that of Mohamed Ezam Mohamed Nor, left, a senior leader of the National Justice Party, which supports the jailed politician Anwar Ibrahim. Mr. Mohamed Ezam said he was being charged under the Official Secrets Act.   (AP)

2000-01-14.title: Mexican Star in Sex Case Is Arrested in Rio
2000-01-14.abstract: Gloria Trevi, pop star in Mexico, and her manager, Sergio Andrade Sanchez, are arrested in Brazil on Mexican charges of corrupting minors; Andrade is accused of forcing sex on young girls who were among his teenage protegees; he and Trevi dropped from view over year ago; scandal has exposed underside of entertainment world filled with freewheeling sex, where Andrade had gained mesmerizing power of cult leader with young girls who hoped for stardom; charges against him and Trevi were first brought by parents of young girl who claim Andrade kidnapped her and fathered her baby by force; photos (M)

2000-01-14.title: Harrods Loses Prince Philip's Seal of Approval
2000-01-14.abstract: Buckingham Palace announces that it is withdrawing royal warrant awarded by Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, to Harrods, London department store that is owned by Mohamed al-Fayed; Fayed, Egyptian-born businessman, has become irritant to royal family over last two years by persistently theorizing that car crash that took lives of his son and Princess Diana was conspiracy planned by British secret security forces to keep Diana from marrying his son; photo (M)

2000-01-15.title: New on the Russian Front Lines: Army Chaplains
2000-01-15.abstract: Russian troops in Chechnya are accompanied by Orthodox Church chaplains who offer comfort and help with personal problems; one priest, Father Safrony, sees himself as heir to pre-Soviet tradition, and says he tells soldiers they are defending sacred Russian land; photos; map (M)

2000-01-15.title: Glaxo and SmithKline Try Again After Costly Failure : Drug Giants Talk Merger
2000-01-15.abstract: LONDON—  Glaxo Wellcome PLC and SmithKline Beecham PLC, whose botched merger attempt two years ago stood out as one of the costliest failures of the 1990s merger boom, said Friday that they had revived talks toward a combination that would create the world's largest pharmaceutical company.

2000-01-15.title: Kursumlija Journal; Serbs at Border Complain of Attacks From Kosovo
2000-01-15.abstract: Serbs in Kursumlija, ten miles from Kosovo border, complain of attacks and land mines by Kosovo Albanians, former members of separatist Kosovo Liberation Army; photo; map; Serbian police report at least 16 attacks on officers and villagers since June (M)

2000-01-15.title: U.S. Fights Tide on a Move to Raise the Military Service Age
2000-01-15.abstract: US finds few international supporters for proposal that any global pact on military service age allow volunteers younger than 18 to fight for their country; US allows volunteer enlistments at age 17 with parental consent; Britain recruits at age 16; human rights campaigners seek minimum age of 18 (S)

2000-01-15.title: War Crimes Court Convicts 5 Bosnian Croats in '93 Massacre
2000-01-15.abstract: International war crimes tribunal in The Hague convicts and sentences five Bosnian Croat militiamen for taking part in massacre of more than 100 Muslims in Bosnian village of Ahmici, in April 1993; court, in largest case to date, finds that killings of Muslims, including children, burned alive in their homes were part of organized terror campaign; five get sentences of six to 25 years (S)

2000-01-16.title: Full Hospitals Make Canadians Wait and Look South
2000-01-16.abstract: Canadian hospitals are overflowing with patients, reflecting strains on nation's state-run health system and causing many hundreds of Canadians to cross border for treatment in US; hospitals are turning away ambulances this winter, and patients are often stacked up in hallways; photo; map; government has both closed door to foreign doctors and cut number of spaces in medical schools; 6,000 nurses and at least 1,000 doctors have moved to US in recent years; hospitals have long waiting lists even for heart surgery and cancer treatmentand critical CAT scans (M)

2000-01-16.title: Dollar May Buy a Reprieve in Ecuador
2000-01-16.abstract: Ecuadoran Pres Jamil Mahuad, facing resignation or ouster in recent weeks, may have bought reprieve with surprise move to replace local currency with US dollar, in hopes of stabilizing economy after months of crisis; photo of demonstrator (M)

2000-01-16.title: France Presses the U.N. to Help Poor Nations Get AIDS Drugs
2000-01-16.abstract: France presses UN to find ways to make AIDS drugs available to poor nations; envoy Alain Dejammeet makes proposals at Security Council meeting on AIDS crisis in Africa (S)

2000-01-16.title: In Chile's Vote Today, Candidates Converge in Center
2000-01-16.abstract: Chilean presidential campaign between Ricardo Lagos, austere Socialist, and Joaquin Lavin, ebullient conservative who began career under Gen Augusto Pinochet, explained; photos (M)

2000-01-16.title: For Serbs in Croatia, a Pledge Unkept
2000-01-16.abstract: Ethnic Serbs who fled Croatia during strife of last decade find they are unable to return because towns like Knin, center of Serb resistance, are almost entirely repopulated by Croats from neighboring Bosnia, themselves refugees from war; Pres Franjo Tudjman had promised to help refugees return, as part of Dayton peace accords, but local housing authorities ignore requests, using red tape to block Serb' return and preventing them from repairing homes; photos (M)

2000-01-17.title: U.S. Officials Pore Over Putin's Resume
2000-01-17.abstract: Clinton administration officials say Vladimir V Putin, Russia's acting president, appears to be a modern decision maker who knows how to make things happen, but they are unsure whether his political instincts match that style of action; their initial take on Putin is that he sees Russia's future as a fairly democratic one, with a market economy of some kind and participation in the world community; photo (M)

2000-01-17.title: Estrongo Nachama, 81, Dies; Chief Cantor of Berlin's Jews
2000-01-17.abstract: Estrongo Nachama, chief cantor of Berlin Jewish community and the man who did as much as anyone to revive Jewish life in the city after Hitler destroyed it, dies at age 81; photo (M)

2000-01-17.title: Warring Sides in Burundi Get a Scolding From Mandela
2000-01-17.abstract: Nelson Mandela, former president of South Africa, begins his duties as mediator for conflict in Burundi with an extraordinary attack on the nation's warring leaders--seated in a long row in front of him--for failing their people and shaming Africa; outlines major points of contention with business-like precision and suggests that he hopes to bring regional and even world leaders into the effort to find peace for Burundi; photo (M)

2000-01-17.title: C.I.A. Tells Clinton An Iranian A-Bomb Can't Be Ruled Out
2000-01-17.abstract: CIA reportedly informs senior Clinton administration officials that Iran may now be able to make a nuclear weapons, touching off a sharp debate about Iran's nuclear capacity and the CIA's ability to monitor it; evaluation is apparently not based on evidence that Iran's indigenous efforts to build a bomb have achieved a breakthrough, but rather on fact that US cannot track with great certainty the increased efforts by Iran to acquire nuclear materials technology in international black market; US has made little headway with efforts to weaken longstanding strategic relationship between Iran and Russia, which is still brimming with stockpiles of nuclear fuel and weapons related technology and has long sold sensitive nuclear and missile technology to Iran; photo (M)

2000-01-17.title: Barak Delays 3rd West Bank Transfer, Angering Palestinians
2000-01-17.abstract: Prime Min Ehud Barak postpones an Israeli withdrawal West Bank until after his meeting with Syrian officials in Washington; Palestinians condemn action; photo (M)

2000-01-18.title: Bomb Explodes in Northern Israeli Town
2000-01-18.abstract: Bomb explodes in garbage can in northern Israeli town of Hadera, injuring 20 people; police suspect members of Islamic Holy War (S)

2000-01-18.title: MAN IN THE NEWS: Ricardo Lagos Escobar; A Chilean Socialist in the Clinton-Blair Mold
2000-01-18.abstract: Man-in-the-News biographical sketch of Chile's President-elect Ricardo Lagos; economist and lawyer is Socialist and longtime political moderate; photo (M)

2000-01-18.title: Annan Picks Ex-Arms Inspector To Head Iraq Monitoring Panel
2000-01-18.abstract: UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan selects Rolf Ekeus, Swedish arms-control expert who found and destroyed large quantities of Iraqi weapons after 1991 Persian Gulf war, to head new commission created to finish job more than year after arms monitoring broke down; action follows failure of Security Council to meet deadline to make nomination and is seen as challenge to Iraq's supporters on council, particularly Russia, China and France, to veto choice; Iraq, longtime critic of Ekeus, ridicules selection; Russia formally registers objection, throwing nomination in doubt; council delegations express concern that Iraq's friends could engage in filibuster on nomination, stalling action on new inspection system and indefinitely postponing any resolution of Iraq crisis (M)

2000-01-18.title: Rebel Leader Wants a Voice In Turkey's National Dialogue
2000-01-18.abstract: Imprisoned Kurdish rebel leader Abdullah Ocalan seeks role in incipient national dialogue about how Turkey should deal with its Kurdish minority following decision by government to suspend his execution; Prime Min Bulent Ecevit says Ocalan can have no role in dialogue; says he is using prison as pulpit to dictate to Turkish government (S)

2000-01-18.title: Agua Prieta Journal; Boom Turns Border to Speed Bump
2000-01-18.abstract: Agua Prieta, gritty Mexican desert town that shares border with Douglas, Ariz, has become busiest border crossing area along 2,000-mile dividing line between US and Mexico for undocumented Mexican immigrants; surge has overwhelmed 26-mile section of border, and Border Patrol has run out of detention space; 14,664 undocumented migrants have been detained since beginning of year, or nearly twice as many as during corresponding period in 1999; photo; chart; map (M)

2000-01-19.title: The Pope Opens a Door, but Not All Christians Feel Welcome
2000-01-19.abstract: Some of world's largest Christian denominations boycott ceremony in Rome celebrating start of Roman Catholic Holy Year because Pope John Paul II is using occasion to highlight importance of indulgences, practice that helped set off Protestant Reformation; gathering at St Paul's Outside the Walls Basilica is one of largest interfaith gatherings since Second Vatican Council, and it is attended by representatives from 22 Christian churches; pope delivers homily on need to repent for divisions within Christianity; photo (M)

2000-01-19.title: AMERICAN TOPICS : Ivy League Isn't Top Path to Riches
2000-01-19.abstract: Is an Ivy League education — which today can cost dollars 120,000 or more — the best investment in terms of future earning capacity? The answer is not as clear as it once appeared.

2000-01-19.title: In Onslaught, Russian Force Enters Grozny
2000-01-19.abstract: Russian forces battle way toward center of Grozny as struggle for control of Chechnya's capital enters even fiercer phase; seek to fracture rebel defenses and seize heart of city; maps (M)

2000-01-19.title: Syrians Shrug Off Lapse in Peace Talks
2000-01-19.abstract: Syrians are confident that peace agreement with Israel will be reached eventually, despite cancellation of second round of talks in Shepherdstown, West Va, because of lack of progress (M)

2000-01-19.title: Milosevic Government Denies Role in Killing of Serbian Warlord
2000-01-19.abstract: Serbian government of Pres Slobodan Milosevic denies role in assassination of Zeljko Raznatovic, notorious Serb paramilitary leader known as Arkan; says Arkan was criminal and was killed by Montenegrin mafia seeking to take over Belgrade; says gunman wounded in attack has been identified as Dusan Gavric, member of Raznatovic's paramilitary group since 1993; promises new developments soon in long-standing police investigation of 1997 gangland-style shooting of Zoran Todorovic, politician who was close associate of Milosevic's wife, Mirjana Markovic, and senior official of her Yugoslav United Left party; his death has also been blamed on Milosevic government (M)

2000-01-20.title: Britain to Shrink the Ulster Constabulary and Broaden Its Ranks
2000-01-20.abstract: British government says Royal Ulster Constabulary, militaristic police force of Northern Ireland, will be reduced in size, its membership broadened to better reflect population of province and its politically charged name retired; Northern Ireland Secretary Peter Mandelson tells boisterous session of Parliament that changes are needed to produce modern, effective police service that draws support and strength from all parts of community (M)

2000-01-20.title: Moscow's Strange Bedfellows
2000-01-20.abstract: News analysis of decision by Russian Pres Vladimir Putin to cut deal with Communists in legislature, leaving pro-democratic factions out of important positions; his willingness to forsake ideological allies and embrace ideological enemies seems aimed at consolidating his power; photo (M)

2000-01-20.title: U.S. Criticizes U.N. Backers Of Iraq's 'No' To Appointee
2000-01-20.abstract: Clinton Clinton administration warns against allowing government of Saddam Hussein to exercise veto power over UN decisions on how to resume arms inspections in Iraq; criticizes members of Security Council blocking Sec Gen Kofi Annan's appointment of Rolf Ekeus as head of new disarmament commission for Iraq; Russia, France and China have objected to return of Ekeus, executive chairman of first disarmament commission for Iraq from creation after Persian Gulf war in 1991 until 1997; say Ekeus, who cooperated closely with US, is unacceptable to Iraqis (S)

2000-01-20.title: Russian Aim: Wind Up the War in Chechnya Before Election
2000-01-20.abstract: Lt Gen Gennadi Troshev, deputy commander of Russian forces in northern Caucasus, says military plans to end campaign in Chechnya by February 26, exactly month before presidential election; statement underscores political imperative of bringing war in breakaway province to early close; Russian forces press assault on Grozny, Chechen capital; are reportadly advancing slowly against smaller but determined group of rebels, who have mined streets and are using small mobile bands of fighters; maps (M)

2000-01-20.title: University, Mexico's Pride, Is Ravaged by Strike
2000-01-20.abstract: National Autonomous University of Mexico, once proud symbol of nation's publicly financed education for masses, is crippled by strike that began in April 1999 as limited student protest seeking to preserve free tuition and open admissions but has spun out of control of administrators, Pres Ernesto Zedillo and even broad-based student organization that initiated it; has suffered losses in students, research, finances and especially academic stature that it will probably never recoup; strike has dragged on because tough, anarchic leftist movement that took control of revolt has run headlong into new world and new Mexico, where shrinking public spending can no longer promote social mobility through huge and generous institutions like national university; photo (M)

2000-01-21.title: Spotlight on Arafat, Capital's Lone Mideast Player
2000-01-21.abstract: Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, with Syrian-Israeli talks in abeyance, has full attention of Pres Clinton and State Sec Madeleine K Albright, capped by lunch with Albright at her Georgetown house (S)

2000-01-21.title: Many Doubt Official Chechnya Toll
2000-01-21.abstract: After four months of war in north Caucasus, serious doubts have been raised about Russian military's official death count--by media, by experts, by soldiers' families and by Russian soldiers themselves, who have taken to yelling out higher death tolls to reporters visiting sites of recent battles (M)

2000-01-21.title: In the Words of Helms: 'A Lack of Gratitude'
2000-01-21.abstract: Excerpts from Sen Jesse Helms's speech to United Nations Security Council (M)

2000-01-21.title: China to End Bank Secrecy In Effort Against Corruption
2000-01-21.abstract: Head of China's central bank describes renewed efforts to turn China's giant state banks and ailing state enterprises into sound commercial operations, able to withstand the competitive pressures that are certain to follow China's entry into World Trade Orgn; Dai Xianglong, head of People's Bank of China, speaking at his annual news conference, also pledges a smooth growth in money supply and a stable exchange rate (M)

2000-01-21.title: Helms, in Visit To U.N., Offers Harsh Message
2000-01-21.abstract: Sen Jesse Helms visits United Nations Security Council offering 'hand of friendship' but delivers sort of clenched-fist message that has made him symbol of right-wing hostility toward United Nations; Helms, North Carolina Republican and most powerful Congressional voice in foreign policy as chmn of Senate Foreign Relations Committee, contends Congress has right to dictate conditions for payment of American debts to organization; warns UN to keep its 'utopian' visions away from American sovereignty (M)

2000-01-22.title: Allende's Daughter Sees a Circle Closing
2000-01-22.abstract: Isabel Allende, daughter of Salvador Allende, celebrates her 55th birthday and election of Ricardo Lagos, first Socialist to win power in Chile since her father died in military coup in 1973; interview; photo; she predicts subdued reaction if former dictator Augusto Pinochet returns from Britain (S)

2000-01-22.title: BIG SMUGGLING RING WITH A WIDE REACH SCANDALIZES CHINA
2000-01-22.abstract: Huge smuggling scandal under investigation in China involves dozens of Communist party, police and banking officials, wife of Beijing's powerful party chief, virtually entire customs department of major seaport of Xiamen and flamboyant corporate figure, Lai Changxing, who has vanished; sprawling syndicate smuggled billions of dollars worth of cars, oil and industrial materials through Xiamen in 1990's; case embarrasses Jiang Zemin's government and suggests critical weaknesses in Communist party and its ability to rule vast country; only hints have been published in official press, but Beijing party officials confirm that Xiamen deputy mayor Lan Fu and wife have fled country; Lin Youfang, wife of Beijing official Jia Qinglin, is detained (M)

2000-01-22.title: Gain for Germany's Far Right Feared
2000-01-22.abstract: Crisis in Germany's Christian Democratic party, which has served to marginalize extreme-right movement, may benefit rightist fringe parties; concern is that ethics accusations against former Chancellor Helmut Kohl and his party could shake relatively shallow roots of German democracy (M)

2000-01-22.title: Agreement Bars Using Children as Soldiers
2000-01-22.abstract: Negotiators in Geneva agree on new international accord barring use of child soldiers in war; US drops opposition to setting 18 as minimum age for sending soldiers into combat, after months of pressure from Pentagon to keep age 17 as option; Joint Chiefs of Staff accept compromise allowing recruitment and training of 17-year-olds as long as 'all feasible measures' are taken to keep them out of combat; new minimum will have little real effect on US military (M)

2000-01-22.title: 2 Bombs, One Lethal, Explode in Madrid; Spain Blames Basques
2000-01-22.abstract: Two car bombs explode in Madrid, killing army officer, Pedro Antonio Blanco Garcia, and injuring other people; government blames Basque separatist group ETA (S)

2000-01-23.title: Ecuador Coup Shifts Control To No. 2 Man
2000-01-23.abstract: Ecuadoran Vice president Gustavo Noboa Bejarno assumes control of government after dissident military officers and Indian groups overthrow elected president Jamil Mahuad; takes office after armed forces chief Gen Carlos Mendoza dissolves three-man junta that briefly led government; Mendoza says move is aimed at preventing international isolation of Ecuador; threats by US and Latin American countries to impose sanctions if constitutional rule was not preserved noted; Noboa becomes sixth chief of state in four years; says he has support of armed forces and national police; Mahuad condemns coup but acknowledges Noboa's authority; coup is Latin America's first overthrow of sitting civilian president in more than decade; comes amid Ecuador's worst economic crisis in more than 70 years; economy contracted by 7.5 percent in 1999, and inflation was more than 80 percent for second consecutive year--worst economic performance in Latin America; country defaulted on half of its $13 billion foreign debt in September 1999; map; photo (M)

2000-01-23.title: An Embarrassment of Riches Hurts Taiwan's Ruling Party
2000-01-23.abstract: Taiwan's Nationalist Party, which has governed for more than 50 years, is in danger of losing presidency in upcoming elections amid allegations of corruption; candidate, Vice Pres Lien Chan, is trailing rivals in close race to succeed Lee Teng-hui, and prospect of victory by opposition has rattled political establishment; has forced Nationalists to make significant concessions that could permanently alter power structure in Taiwan, regardless of who wins election on March 18; photos; maps (M)

2000-01-23.title: No Headline
2000-01-23.abstract: Ruhiyyih Rabbani, who became pre-eminent member of Bahai faith after death of her husband, last official leader of faith, dies at 89; photo (M)

2000-01-23.title: Arrests in Bishop's Killing Follow Guatemalan Pledge
2000-01-23.abstract: Guatemalan police arrest father and son military officers, retired Col Byron Disrael Lima Estrada and Capt Byron Lima Oliva for murder in 1998 death of Roman Catholic bishop and vigorous human rights advocate Juan Jose Gerardi; arrests make good on pledge by new Pres Alfonso Portillo to bring killers to justice and is victory for human rights groups in Guatemala, which conducted own investigation and have long pointed to Limas as suspects (M)

2000-01-23.title: Rylstone Journal; The Stately 'Calendar Girls' Dressed So Simply in Pearls
2000-01-23.abstract: Eleven members of Rylstone, England, chapter of Women's Institute, aged 45 to 66, provoke wide-eyed interest by posing nude and substituting photos on their annual calendar, instead of usual images of watery sunsets and hiking paths bursting with buttercups, in ploy to raise money for leukemia research; wholesome rural service organization, better known for 'jam and Jerusalem'--home of bottled preserves from English country kitchens and Blake poem enshrining 'England's green and pleasant land'--raises $550,000 from calender sales, surpassing projected $2,000, and even wins unexpected praise from thousands of middle-aged women who say calender has restored their own flagging self-esteem; photos; map (M)

2000-01-24.title: Beltway on Display: American Politics at the U.N.
2000-01-24.abstract: News analysis of UN Amb Richard C Holbrooke's success in giving world body high degree of visibility among Washington's movers and shakers; Holbrooke's carefully orchestrated meetings and nonstop hospitality to influential members of Congress may have made UN safe again for American politicians (M)

2000-01-24.title: President Of Israel Faces Accusations With Defiance
2000-01-24.abstract: Pres Ezer Weizman of Israel defiantly rebuts calls by political allies and opponents, as well as news media, to resign or step down temporarily while police pursue criminal investigation of his receipt of hundreds of thousands of dollars from French businessman, Edouard Saroussi; denies in televised address that he broke law or otherwise engaged in wrongdoing; says he will issue statement about facts of affair when investigation is over (M)

2000-01-24.title: Burmese Rebels Seize Hostages in Thailand
2000-01-24.abstract: Armed rebels from Burmese insurgent group called God's Army reportedly cross border into Thailand and seize local hospital, taking as many as 200 people hostage and demanding medical care for their wounded (S)

2000-01-24.title: A Million Protesters in Spain Denounce Terrorism's Return
2000-01-24.abstract: Approximately one million people march in Madrid, Spain, in protest against Basque separatist group ETA, suspected in two car bombings in city in which army officer died; march is led by Prime Min Jose Maria Aznar and three former prime ministers and caps weekend of nationwide protests; bombings appear to reignite separatist violence after 18-month lull; photo (M)

2000-01-24.title: Japan Sect's Name Change Brings Confusion and Fear
2000-01-24.abstract: Decision by Japan's Aum Shinrikyo sect to denounce spiritual leader Shoko Asahara for role in 1991 nerve gas attack against Tokyo subway system that killed 12 people, and to change its name to Aleph appears to have raised more fears than it calmed; organizations that use name Aleph have been flooded with telephone calls from people concerned that they are associated with sect; photo (M)

2000-01-25.title: Russia Says Cost of Chechen War Far Exceeds Estimate
2000-01-25.abstract: Russia's first deputy prime minister Mikhail Kasaynov acknowledges that Kremlin underestimated cost of Chechen war and spent 50 percent more in 1999 than initially planned; says government budgeted $125 million, but instead spent $178 million; soaring economic and military costs reflect intensity of fighting in Chechnya and inability of Russian forces to bring conflict to early close; are expected to sharpen Western debate over providing credits and other forms of economic assistance to Russia; photos (M)

2000-01-25.title: Party Audit Finds Shortfall in Kohl Era
2000-01-25.abstract: FRANKFURT—  Germany's scandal-shaken conservative party, which Monday confirmed that it cannot account for more than 12 million Deutsche marks in political funds, said that it was mired in an "existential trial" and would begin a national campaign to win back public trust.

2000-01-25.title: Way Clear for U.S. to Deliver Rwanda War Crimes Suspect
2000-01-25.abstract: Supreme Court clears way for US to send Rwandan church leader, Elizaphan Ntakirutimana, to war crimes tribunal, where he is wanted on charges of abetting genocide in 1994 for allowing armed Hutus to enter his church and kill hundreds of Tutsis who sought refuge there; he was arrested in Texas in 1996 by federal marshals acting under agreement between US and war crimes tribunals for Balkans and Rwanda, but judge ruled agreement, subsequently enacted into law later in year, unconstitutional and ordered him released in 1997 (M)

2000-01-25.title: Audit Traces Illegal Funds In Kohl Party
2000-01-25.abstract: Audit of Germany's Christian Democratic Union shows more than $6.3 million in illicit contributions under former Chancellor Helmut Kohl, suggesting system of secret financing far greater than previously ackowledged; audit by Ernst & Young provides no clues to sources of payments to Kohl's party that he has refused to reveal; shows $1 million Kohl admits receiving from 1993 to 1998 followed earlier and much larger payments (M)

2000-01-25.title: Nafah Journal; Now Shooting on Golan: A War Movie
2000-01-25.abstract: Interview with Amos Gitai, one of Israel's few internationally acclaimed filmmakers, about movie Kippur--his deeply personal film about warfare on Golan Heights during 1973 Arab-Israeli war; photo; map (M)

2000-01-26.title: Pakistanis Lost Control Of Militants, U.S. Hints
2000-01-26.abstract: Harkat ul-Mujahedeen, militant group believed responsible for hijacking of Indian Airlines jet in December, emerged in early 1990's as movement dedicated to Kashmir operations against Indian troops and civilian targets; US believes group is supported by Pakistani intelligence service and is extending its influence as militant Islamic political parties gain power in Pakistan; State Department's 1999 report on global terrorism linked group, under Fazlur Rehman Khalil, to Osama bin Laden; group operated terrorist training camps in Afghanistan and suffered casualties during US missile strikes aganst bin Laden in 1998; Khalil warned of revenge; Pakistani leader Gen Pervez Musharraf has turned down request by three senior Clinton administration officials to shut down group; officials were in Pakistan to assess prospects for stopover by Pres Clinton during trip to region in March (M)

2000-01-26.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2000-01-26.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: MARRIAGE AND DIVORCE -- A high-ranking Church of England committee recommended that the divorced be permitted to remarry even if former spouses are still living. The move would formalize what is now common practice and would pave the way for the Prince of Wales to marry his longtime companion, Camilla Parker Bowles, left, an event previously seen as impossible for a future supreme governor of the church. To take effect, the recommendation must be adopted by the church Synod, which meets next year.   Warren Hoge (NYT)    BRITAIN: BELGIUM'S PINOCHET PLEA -- Belgium joined six rights groups in a move in the High Court to keep Britain from letting Gen. Augusto Pinochet, 84, return home to Chile. Belgium is seeking an order blocking his departure until he is examined by Belgian doctors. Home Secretary Jack Straw has refused to make public a report by four British doctors that found the former dictator medically unfit to stand trial in Spain on torture charges. Brussels has filed an extradition bid in behalf of Chileans living in Belgium.   Warren Hoge (NYT)

2000-01-26.title: Colombian Asks Congress for Aid Not Tied to Human Rights
2000-01-26.abstract: Colombian Pres Andres Pastrana visits Washington and begins campaigning for Congress to approve more than billion dollars in emergency aid not tied to his military's actions on human rights; meets with Pres Clinton and says in interview that, while his government is committed to improving rights record, efforts will not be helped by further US pressure; photo (M)

2000-01-26.title: Turkish Terror Victim Espoused a Tolerant Islam
2000-01-26.abstract: Turkey is in shock over discovery of dozens of savagely tortured bodies beneath hide-outs of religious terror group Hizbullah or Party of God, which aims to overthrow secular state and establish Islamic republic; outrage and grief focuses on Konca Kuris, writer often described as Muslim feminist; she was kidnapped in front of her home in July 1998 and not seen again until burned, disfigured body was exhumed from shallow grave in Koyna, one of 33 found so far at Hizbullah properties; photo; videotapes of her torture also reported found; most of other victims apparently were Kurdish businessmen from war-torn provinces in southeast (S)

2000-01-26.title: Swiss Freeze A Dictator's Giant Cache
2000-01-26.abstract: GENEVA—  Switzerland is confronting the largest dirty money investigation in its history after disclosures that dollars 654 million has been found in accounts in the names of the former Nigerian dictator Sani Abacha, his family and associates.

2000-01-27.title: EVIDENCE IS SEEN LINKING BIN LADEN TO ALGERIAN GROUP
2000-01-27.abstract: US investigators have uncovered what they believe are links between Algerians who have been charged with plotting terrorist attack in US and Osama bin Laden, exiled Saudi accused of bombing two US Embassies in East Africa; authorities in Senegal recently arrested Mohambedou Ould Slahi; US investigators believe that he directed Algerian group in Canada in its efforts to enter US and carry out bomb plot late last year; say Slahi is brother-in-law of one of bin Laden's key lieutenants, but they refuse to identify him; bomb plot was foiled when border agents in Port Angeles, Wash, on Dec 14 arrested Ahmed Ressam, Algerian who was driving car loaded with explosives (M)

2000-01-27.title: Syria Businessmen Yearn for Reforms
2000-01-27.abstract: Young Syrian entrepreneurs like Hashem Akkad, legislator and businessman, are eager to catch up with their contemporaries in other Arab countries; say Pres Hafez al-Assad's economic policies are built on socialism and cronyism and should be liberalized; photo (M)

2000-01-27.title: U.N. Urges End to Congo Cease-Fire Impasse
2000-01-27.abstract: United Nations Security Council, laying groundwork for eventual United Nations peacekeeping operation in Congo, urges African nations involved in war to put into effect peace accord they negotiated last summer (M)

2000-01-27.title: Mexico Gives Itself High Marks for Last Year's Anti-Drug Effort
2000-01-27.abstract: Mexican government asserts that its soldiers and police seized far more drugs in 1999 than in previous years, partly as result of increased spending on high-tech gadgetry, aircraft and naval vessels deployed in narcotics war (M)

2000-01-27.title: Syrians Not Counting On Peace Dividends
2000-01-27.abstract: While Syria and Israel have been discussing an end to their 52-year state of war, many Syrian businessmen fear they would not reap any economic dividends of peace, despite its promise of access to Western markets and investment.     Instead, these restless executives, fully equipped with luxury cars, marble-floored offices, satellite dishes, tailored suits and high-end computers, say that the question of whether they will do business with Israelis after a peace treaty is irrelevant.

2000-01-28.title: Wiesel Urges Germany to Ask Forgiveness
2000-01-28.abstract: Germany ends decade of turbulent debate over how to commemorate Holocaust in its new capital by dedicating a vast site beside the Brandenburg Gate for a memorial designed by New York architect Peter Eisenman; most of German government stands in icy winds beneath new billboards announcing 'Memorial to the Murdered Jews of Europe' as Wolfgang Thierse, speaker of Parliament, explains that honor has to be accorded to six million Jewish dead 'in the heart of our capital city'; construction will begin next year on 4.9-acre site; field of more than 2,000 stone pillars of varying dimensions will be flanked by 'documentation center' whose precise role remains unclear; resistant to the last, Eberhard Diepgen, Berlin's Christian Democratic mayor, snubs ceremony; Germany, to complete its formal remembrance of Holocaust, on 55th anniversary of liberation of Auschwitz, invites Elie Wiesel, Nobel Peace laureate, to address lower house of Parliament; photos (L)

2000-01-28.title: U.S. Warns Pakistan It May Be Branded a Sponsor of Terrorism
2000-01-28.abstract: Clinton adm issues public warning to Pakistan that it could be branded a state sponsor of terrorism--ending any hope for a resumption of large-scale American aid--if Pakistani Army continues to support terrorists blamed for hijacking of Indian Airlines jet last month (S)

2000-01-28.title: Talks on Biotech Food Turn on a Safety Principle
2000-01-28.abstract: Delegates at global biosafety conference in Montreal debate 'precautionary principle' as they strive to negotiate world's first treaty regulating trade in genetically modified products; negotiators ask whether a nation must have scientific proof before it can ban a genetically engineered crop or animal, or can it act even in absence of scientific certainty (M)

2000-01-28.title: Bill Widens Gulf in Japan
2000-01-28.abstract: Governing coalition in Japan, in move that sets stage for major political showdown, pushes through bill to eliminate 20 seats from 500-seat lower house of Parliament; all 20 are from 200 seats chosen by proportional representation, and opposition politicians say move would favor Prime Min Keizo Obuchi's coalition (S)

2000-01-28.title: Russians Agree to End Parliament Walkout
2000-01-28.abstract: Leaders of three Russian political parties agree to end their boycott of Russian Parliament on Feb 9, thereby avoiding an open-ended confrontation with acting president, Vladimir V Putin, and his parliamentary allies (M)

2000-01-29.title: Global Elite Are Fretting On Response To Backlash
2000-01-29.abstract: DAVOS, Switzerland—  When the talk is of digital convergence or financial markets, the optimism, even euphoria, is palpable throughout the conference halls and corridors of the annual meeting of the World Economic Forum now under way in this chic Alpine ski resort.

2000-01-29.title: Ex-Official In France Again Faces Investigation
2000-01-29.abstract: Dominique Strauss-Kahn, a former French finance minister, is being investigated in connection with $29,000 that his secretary, Evelyne Duval, received from the oil company Elf Aquitaine in 1993 (M)

2000-01-29.title: South Africa Codifies Ban on Bias In Constitution
2000-01-29.abstract: South Africa's Parliament passes far-reaching bill explicity outlawing discrimination on basis of race, ethnicity and sex; bill would also ban discrimination against homosexuals, pregnant women and the disabled and bar malicious use of derogatory labels for blacks and other people of color; protection extended in earlier draft to poor people, foreigners and people infected with HIV is removed after protests from business leaders and others (M)

2000-01-29.title: World Briefing
2000-01-29.abstract: EUROPE    THE HAGUE: WAR CRIMES CASE OPENS -- Mitar Vasiljevic, a Bosnian Serb accused of killing 135 Muslim men, women and children by locking them inside two houses in Visigrad in June 1992 and setting the buildings on fire, made his first court appearance at the Yugoslavia war crimes tribunal. Mr. Vasiljevic, a 45-year-old former paramilitary fighter, was arrested by NATO forces on Tuesday. He pleaded not guilty.   Marlise Simons
